message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 21
Recent changes: Added PROCESSING_FEE constant and +5 at end of return expression. Updated all prior test expectations.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

content.text: I'll start the Refactor phase by reading the necessary files.

content.content:
1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted inline `{ type: string }` to exported `type Item` for function signature clarity
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- `items: Item[]` reads better than `items: { type: string }[]`
6	- Naming established: `Item` (type alias for item objects), `calculateBasePremium` (function)
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: replaced if/else conditional with `BASE_PREMIUM` lookup table (`Record<string, number>`)
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- declarative map makes type-to-premium mapping explicit and treats all item types symmetrically
11	- Naming established: `BASE_PREMIUM` (module-level constant mapping item type to base premium value)
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: added `Readonly` wrapper to `BASE_PREMIUM` type annotation
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- communicates immutability of configuration constant and enables compile-time mutation prevention
16	- Naming established: --
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- No refactoring (justified): attempted destructuring `items[0]` into named binding but reverted -- mass increased from 8 to 15 with only marginal clarity gain; code already at local optimum after 3 prior refinement cycles
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- Change: replaced `Readonly<Record<string, number>>` with `as const` assertion and derived `ItemType = keyof typeof BASE_PREMIUM` union type
23	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- `Item.type` is now a precise union instead of `string`, enabling compile-time validation of item types
24	- Naming established: `ItemType` (union type: `"sword" | "amulet" | "staff" | "potion" | "rune"`)
25	
26	## After cycle 6
27	- Change: replaced imperative `for` loop + mutable `sum` with `items.reduce(...)` single expression
28	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- reduce expresses "fold to sum" directly and eliminates 2 assignments (mass 28 -> 18)
29	- Naming established: --
30	
31	## After cycle 7
32	- Change: extracted `isComponentBlock(items)` helper, introduced `BLOCK_SIZE`, `BLOCK_PREMIUM`, and `COMPONENT_TYPES` named constants
33	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- inline condition `items.length === 3 && items.every(...) && BASE_PREMIUM[...] === 25` was opaque; extracted function makes block-detection logic self-documenting; mass +5 justified by clarity gain
34	- Naming established: `isComponentBlock` (predicate), `BLOCK_SIZE` (3), `BLOCK_PREMIUM` (60), `COMPONENT_TYPES` (ReadonlySet of component item types eligible for block discount)
35	
36	## After cycle 8
37	- Change: restructured `isComponentBlock` with early-return guard clause and extracted `blockType` named binding from repeated `items[0].type`
38	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- guard clause separates precondition from core logic; `blockType` names the concept more clearly than indexed access; mass +1 justified by clarity
39	- Naming established: `blockType` (local binding in `isComponentBlock` for the type that defines the block)
40	
41	## After cycle 9
42	- Change: removed dead `isComponentBlock` function (no longer called after green phase replaced it with inline group-by-type block detection in `calculateBasePremium`)
43	- Rationale: Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- dead code adds cognitive load and 11 mass units for zero value
44	- Naming established: --
45	
46	## After cycle 10
47	- Change: merged cursed surcharge loop into item iteration loop; introduced `cursedSurcharge` named accumulator separate from `total`
48	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- named separation of base premiums (`total`) vs item-specific modifiers (`cursedSurcharge`) clarifies composition; reduced loops from 3 to 2
49	- Naming established: `cursedSurcharge` (accumulator for item-level cursed modifier, kept separate from base premium total)
50	
51	## After cycle 11
52	- Change: extracted `itemSurcharge(item: Item): number` helper; collapsed `cursedSurcharge`/`enchantmentSurcharge` into single `totalSurcharge` accumulator in main function
53	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- separates per-item surcharge logic from grouping/block-discount logic; names the concept; simplifies main loop body from 3 accumulators + 2 conditionals to 1 accumulator + 1 call
54	- Naming established: `itemSurcharge` (function computing all item-level surcharges for one item), `totalSurcharge` (accumulator in main function)
55	
56	## After cycle 12
57	- Change: renamed `calculateBasePremium` to `calculatePremium`; extracted `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2` and `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2` as named constants
58	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- function now computes full premium with policy-wide modifiers, not just base; magic numbers replaced with self-documenting constants
59	- Naming established: `calculatePremium` (renamed from `calculateBasePremium`), `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` (2), `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE` (0.2)
60	
61	## After cycle 13
62	- Change: extracted inline magic numbers in `itemSurcharge` to named constants `CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.3)
63	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- consistent with existing pattern of named rate/threshold constants; mass +24 justified by clarity
64	- Naming established: `CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.3)
65	
66	## After cycle 14
67	- Change: replaced mutable accumulator (`let surcharge = 0; surcharge += ...`) in `itemSurcharge` with immutable named bindings (`const cursed`, `const enchantment`) and direct return
68	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- named bindings make surcharge composition explicit; eliminating 3 assignments reduced mass from 43 to 29
69	- Naming established: `cursed` (local const in `itemSurcharge` for curse surcharge amount), `enchantment` (local const for high-enchantment surcharge amount)
70

content.text:
All 21 tests pass. Now I'll proceed with the refactor phase systematically.

## Step 0: Architecture Notes Loaded

I've read `architecture-notes.md` -- 14 prior cycles of refinement are documented. The most recent change (cycle 14) replaced mutable accumulators in `itemSurcharge` with immutable bindings.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

**Naming Evaluation**:
- Current name: `calculatePremium`
- Function purpose: computes the full insurance premium for a list of items, accounting for base premiums, component block discounts, item surcharges (cursed/enchantment), loyalty discount, first-insurance surcharge, follow-up discount, and a processing fee.
- Question: Does "calculatePremium" clearly reveal this intent?
- Assessment: Yes -- the function calculates the premium for a policy quote. The name accurately describes what it does.
- Decision: Keep current name because `calculatePremium` clearly conveys the purpose.

- Current name: `itemSurcharge`
- Function purpose: computes per-item surcharges (cursed and high-enchantment) based on the item's base premium.
- Assessment: Clear and descriptive.
- Decision: Keep.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Let me calculate the current mass of the implementation.

**Current Code Mass for `itemSurcharge`:**
```
function itemSurcharge(item: Item): number {
  const base = BASE_PREMIUM[item.type];           // binding(base):1, invocation(bracket-access):2, binding(item):1
  const cursed = item.cursed ? base * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE : 0;  // binding(cursed):1, conditional:4, invocation(*):2, const(0):1
  const enchantment =
    item.enchantment !== undefined && item.enchantment >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD
      ? base * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE
      : 0;                                        // binding(enchantment):1, conditional:4, invocations(!==, >=, &&, *):8, const(undefined, 0):2
  return cursed + enchantment;                     // invocation(+):2
}
```
`itemSurcharge` mass:
- Constants: 3 (undefined, 0, 0) = 3
- Bindings: 4 (item, base, cursed, enchantment) = 4
- Invocations: 7 (bracket-access, *, !==, &&, >=, *, +) = 14
- Conditionals: 2 (ternary, ternary) = 8
- Loops: 0 = 0
- Assignments: 0 = 0
- Subtotal: 29

**Current Code Mass for `calculatePremium`:**
```
function calculatePremium(items: Item[], customer?, options?): number {
  const groups = new Map<ItemType, number>();        // binding(groups):1, invocation(new Map):2
  let totalSurcharge = 0;                            // binding(totalSurcharge):1, const(0):1, assignment:6
  for (const item of items) {                        // loop:5, binding(item):1
    groups.set(item.type, (groups.get(item.type) ?? 0) + 1); // invocations(set, get, ??, +):8, const(0, 1):2
    totalSurcharge += itemSurcharge(item);            // assignment:6, invocation(itemSurcharge, +=):4
  }

  let total = 0;                                     // binding(total):1, const(0):1, assignment:6
  for (const [type, count] of groups) {              // loop:5, bindings(type, count):2
    if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === BLOCK_SIZE) { // conditional:4, invocations(has, &&, ===):6
      total += BLOCK_PREMIUM;                        // assignment:6
    } else {
      total += count * BASE_PREMIUM[type];           // assignment:6, invocations(*, bracket-access):4
    }
  }
  let policyPremium = total + totalSurcharge;        // binding(policyPremium):1, assignment:6, invocation(+):2
  if (customer && customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD) { // conditional:4, invocations(&&, >=):4
    policyPremium *= 1 - LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE;      // assignment:6, invocations(*, -):4, const(1):1
  }
  policyPremium += policyPremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE; // assignment:6, invocations(+, *):4
  if (options?.isFollowUp) {                         // conditional:4, invocation(?.):2
    policyPremium *= 1 - FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE;    // assignment:6, invocations(*, -):4, const(1):1
  }
  return policyPremium + PROCESSING_FEE;             // invocation(+):2
}
```

`calculatePremium` mass:
- Constants: 6 (three 0s, two 1s, one 1) = 6
- Bindings: 7 (items, customer, options, groups, totalSurcharge, item, total, type, count, policyPremium) -- actually 10 = 10
- Invocations: 20 (new Map, set, get, ??, +, itemSurcharge, +=, has, &&, ===, *, bracket, +, &&, >=, *, -, +=, *, ?., *, -, +) -- let me recount carefully = ~20 => 40
- Conditionals: 3 = 12
- Loops: 2 = 10
- Assignments: 7 (let totalSurcharge=0, +=, let total=0, +=, +=, let policyPremium=, *=, +=, *=) -- 9 assignments = 54

Total `calculatePremium` mass: 6 + 10 + 40 + 12 + 10 + 54 = 132

Combined mass (both functions): 29 + 132 = 161

(Approximate -- the exact number isn't critical; what matters is the direction of change.)

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

### Rule 1: Tests Pass
All 21 tests currently passing.

### Rule 2: Reveals Intent
Looking at the `calculatePremium` function, I notice the sequence of policy-wide modifiers is applied imperatively with mutable reassignments:

```typescript
let policyPremium = total + totalSurcharge;
if (customer && customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD) {
  policyPremium *= 1 - LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE;
}
policyPremium += policyPremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE;
if (options?.isFollowUp) {
  policyPremium *= 1 - FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE;
}
return policyPremium + PROCESSING_FEE;
```

This uses 4 mutable reassignments on `policyPremium`. The first-insurance surcharge line `policyPremium += policyPremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` can be simplified to `policyPremium *= 1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` which is both clearer (same form as the others -- multiplying by a factor) and eliminates one invocation.

This is a small but meaningful refactoring: making the first-insurance surcharge consistent with the other multiplicative modifiers (loyalty and follow-up). All three become `*= (1 +/- RATE)` which reveals a pattern: each policy modifier is a multiplicative factor applied in sequence.

### Rule 3: No Duplication
The three policy modifiers now follow a consistent pattern after this change. No other duplication spotted.

### Rule 4: Fewest Elements
The change also reduces one invocation (the separate `+` in `policyPremium += policyPremium * ...` becomes a single `*=`), reducing mass slightly.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

Let me apply the change.

content.text: The tests failed due to floating-point precision differences. The expressions `policyPremium += policyPremium * rate` and `policyPremium *= 1 + rate` are not exactly equivalent in IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic. I need to revert this change.

content.text:
Good, reverted successfully, all 21 tests pass again. The `*= (1 + rate)` refactoring causes floating-point precision issues. Let me try a different refactoring.

Let me re-examine the code for other opportunities. Let me look at the `calculatePremium` function more carefully.

Looking at this code, I see a potential Rule 2 improvement. On line 53, the condition `customer && customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` has a redundant truthiness check. The `customer &&` guard is needed to avoid `undefined.yearsWithMHPCO`, but this can be expressed more clearly with optional chaining: `customer?.yearsWithMHPCO !== undefined && customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`. Actually, looking more closely, the current pattern `customer && customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= ...` is already idiomatic JavaScript. Let me find a better improvement.

I notice that the function signature on line 36 is quite long. But that's a formatting concern, not a Simple Design issue.

Let me look at a different angle. The `customer` parameter type is defined inline as `{ yearsWithMHPCO: number }`. Following the pattern established in cycle 1 (extracting `Item` type for clarity), I could extract a `Customer` type alias. This would improve Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) by naming the concept and making the function signature more readable.

input.old_string: export type Item = { type: ItemType; cursed?: boolean; enchantment?: number };
input.new_string:
export type Item = { type: ItemType; cursed?: boolean; enchantment?: number };
export type Customer = { yearsWithMHPCO: number };

content.text: All 21 tests pass. Let me verify the final state of the implementation.

Good. Now let me calculate the new mass and document the refactoring.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The change is purely at the type level -- extracting `Customer` and `QuoteOptions` type aliases from inline object types. This does not change the runtime code at all, so the mass of the executable code is unchanged. However, the function signature is now significantly more readable: `(items: Item[], customer?: Customer, options?: QuoteOptions)` vs the previous `(items: Item[], customer?: { yearsWithMHPCO: number }, options?: { isFollowUp?: boolean })`.

Mass change: 161 -> 161 (no change in runtime code mass; type aliases are compile-time only).

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Attempted: `*= (1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE)` -- REVERTED due to floating-point precision breakage (11 tests failed)
- Applied: Extracted `Customer` and `QuoteOptions` type aliases from inline object types in `calculatePremium` signature
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Named type aliases make the function signature self-documenting; consistent with the `Item` type extraction established in cycle 1
- Mass unchanged: Type aliases are compile-time constructs

Benefits:
- Function signature reads as `(items: Item[], customer?: Customer, options?: QuoteOptions)` -- each parameter's domain concept is immediately clear
- Consistent with established codebase pattern (`Item`, `ItemType`)
- Types are exported for potential reuse by callers

## Step 8: Append to Architecture Notes

input.old_string:
- Change: replaced mutable accumulator (`let surcharge = 0; surcharge += ...`) in `itemSurcharge` with immutable named bindings (`const cursed`, `const enchantment`) and direct return
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- named bindings make surcharge composition explicit; eliminating 3 assignments reduced mass from 43 to 29
- Naming established: `cursed` (local const in `itemSurcharge` for curse surcharge amount), `enchantment` (local const for high-enchantment surcharge amount)
input.new_string:
- Change: replaced mutable accumulator (`let surcharge = 0; surcharge += ...`) in `itemSurcharge` with immutable named bindings (`const cursed`, `const enchantment`) and direct return
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- named bindings make surcharge composition explicit; eliminating 3 assignments reduced mass from 43 to 29
- Naming established: `cursed` (local const in `itemSurcharge` for curse surcharge amount), `enchantment` (local const for high-enchantment surcharge amount)

## After cycle 15
- Change: extracted inline `{ yearsWithMHPCO: number }` and `{ isFollowUp?: boolean }` to exported `Customer` and `QuoteOptions` type aliases
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- named types make `calculatePremium` signature self-documenting; consistent with `Item` type extraction from cycle 1
- Naming established: `Customer` (type for customer parameter), `QuoteOptions` (type for quote options parameter)
